request.question: Which arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia whose close living relatives are the wombats does Springwood State High School have?

title: Guan (bird)
Spotted elachura
Springwood State High School
Wakaleo vanderleuri
Koala
Rhagophthalmidae
Palorchestes
Centenary Heights State High School
Didunculus
Itatiella
sentences: The guans are a number of bird genera which make up the largest group in the family Cracidae.
They are found mainly in northern South America, southern Central America, and a few adjacent Caribbean islands.
There is also the peculiar horned guan ("Oreophasis derbianus") which is not a true guan, but a very distinct and ancient cracid with no close living relatives (Pereira "et al." 2002).
The spotted elachura or spotted wren-babbler ("Elachura formosa") is a species of passerine bird found in the forests of the eastern Himalayas and Southeast Asia.

In the past it was included in the babbler genus "Spelaeornis" as "S. formosus", but molecular phylogenetic studies in 2014 provided evidence that it was distinct from the babblers and part of a basal lineage (one that diverged early) with no other close living relatives within the passerine bird clade Passerida.
This led to the creation of a new family, Elachuridae, to accommodate just one species (a monotypic taxon).
Springwood State High School is a Queensland state school located in the Logan City suburb of Springwood.
The school opened in 1977 to Grade 8 students with its official opening in 1978.
Springwood State High School is situated on 55 acre of bushland and includes a lagoon which is used by senior Biology students.
In addition to this, the school is home to many native animal species including Koalas, Wallabies and many species of birds.
Wakaleo vanderleuri is a species of marsupial lion that lived in Australia during the Miocene (about 16 to 10million years ago).
Being a marsupial, it is not closely related to true cats; its closest living relatives are wombats and the koala.
This diprotodont is hypothesized to have been an omnivore, eating significant amounts of meat, perhaps more like a bear in diet and habit than like an actual lion.
It lacked large canine-like fangs, producing some controversy regarding just how much it actually preyed on living animals.
The meat-eating hypothesis is based largely upon the morphology of its molar teeth.
The species is believed to have been able to hunt by leaping from trees onto its prey.
The koala ("Phascolarctos cinereus", or, inaccurately, koala bear ) is an arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia.
It is the only extant representative of the family Phascolarctidae and its closest living relatives are the wombats.
The koala is found in coastal areas of the mainland's eastern and southern regions, inhabiting Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia.
It is easily recognisable by its stout, tailless body and large head with round, fluffy ears and large, spoon-shaped nose.
The koala has a body length of 60 – and weighs 4 – .
Pelage colour ranges from silver grey to chocolate brown.
Koalas from the northern populations are typically smaller and lighter in colour than their counterparts further south.
These populations possibly are separate subspecies, but this is disputed.
The Rhagophthalmidae are a family of beetles within the larger Elateroidea group that include click-beetles.

Members of this beetle family have bioluminescent organs on the larvae and are closely related to the Phengodidae (glowworm beetles) and Lampyridae (fireflies), and were often included in one of these families as a subfamily—Rhagophthalminae.
Towards the end of the 20th century, they were increasingly seen as a distinct family.
But they might be the only reasonably close living relatives of the mysterious fireflies of genus "Pterotus", and could eventually end up in a Lampyridae subfamily with these.
They were mainly included in the Phengodidae for having similar adaptation for displaying glowing lights.
However, this seems to be due to convergent evolution.

If they indeed belong in the Lampyridae, they are related to a group of families that have either a distinct type of flashing lights (Luciolinae) or rely exclusively or nearly so on pheromones for communication (the others).
Palorchestes ('ancient leaper or dancer') is an extinct genus of terrestrial herbivorous marsupial of the family Palorchestidae.
The genus was endemic to Australia, living from the Late Miocene subepoch through the Pleistocene epoch (around 11.6 mya – 11,000 years ago), and thought to be in existence for approximately .

Centenary Heights State High School is a State High School located in the suburb of Centenary Heights in Toowoomba, Queensland, with an enrolment of around 1500 students (2015) and was one of Queensland's leading high schools with 11 OP's 1 (Overall Position) scored in 2012.
The school is one of several state high schools in Toowoomba, including Toowoomba State High School , Harristown State High School and Wilsonton State High School.
The tooth-billed pigeons are the only genus ("Didunculus") of the subfamily Didunculinae, in the pigeon and dove family, (Columbidae).
It has no close living relatives, but it has been shown to be genetically close to the dodo, and the genus name "Didunculus" means "little dodo".

The jaw and tongue structure, and the superficially parrotlike bill have suggested a relationship to the parrots, but these features have arisen from its specialised diet rather than any real relationship.
Itatiella ulei is a species of moss in the family Polytrichaceae.
It is the only species in the genus Itatiella.
The Polytrichaceae is a common family of mosses that does not have close living relatives.
Its small size and the inflexed leaf apex characterize "Itatiella ulei".
When this species grows directly exposed to sun at high elevations, it presents a similar aspect but can be distinguished based on the distal lamella cells which are single and rhombic.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- If summary_1 already includes explicit profession/role/descriptor tokens for an anchor (for example, 'singer', 'director', 'country music artist'), omit those redundant descriptor tokens and emit a compact anchor-only query consisting of the core visible anchors (names, titles, groups). Retain relation tokens only when the question explicitly requests them or when disambiguation rules demand them. Do not remove descriptors when the question explicitly asks for role/occupation evidence or when a short-title disambiguation rule requires adding an entertainment disambiguator.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity (or otherwise names candidate/bridge entities), treat the context claim as the summary anchor and perform explicit identity-alignment checks before using passage facts. For each entity explicitly named in `context` as a candidate or bridge, do the following: if at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append a single brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that linking fact and references the corroborating passage and also include one short clause for that context-named entity giving the passage-supported relation value (e.g., "Name: [relation value]."); if no passage provides the relation value for that context-named entity, append a short clause stating that no relation value is provided in the evidence (e.g., "Name: no [relation] provided in evidence."). Do not create such clauses for entities that are only mentioned in `passages` and are not named in `context`. Do not append corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep these additions short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.
- Vocal-role single-token normalization (narrow scope): If summary_2 (alone or composed with summary_1 where identity links are explicit) explicitly contains vocal-role evidence — i.e., one or more verbalized vocal-role phrases that clearly denote a singing performer (examples: "lead singer", "lead vocals", "vocalist" as presented in the summaries) — then you may prefer to emit the concise single-token common noun for a person who sings (e.g., "singer") only when that single-token common noun itself appears verbatim in the summaries or when the vocal-role phrases in the summaries unambiguously denote the same person-role. If those explicit lexical licenses are not present, retain the literal role phrasing as given in summary_2 and do not normalize modifiers or drop qualifiers.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Which arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia whose close living relatives are the wombats does Springwood State High School have?
hop1_docs: Wakaleo | Wakaleo (indigenous Australian "waka", "little", "small", and Latin "leo", "lion") was a genus of medium-sized thylacoleonids that lived in Australia in the early to late Miocene.  It was approximately 2.5 ft (80 cm) long, or the size of a dog.  Although much smaller than its close relative, the marsupial lion "(Thylacoleo carnifex)", "Wakaleo" would have been a successful hunter in its time.  It had teeth specially designed for cutting and stabbing.  The ocelot-sized predator "Wakaleo", along with its jaguar-sized relative "Thylacoleo", were actually related to the herbivore wombats.

Koala | The koala ("Phascolarctos cinereus", or, inaccurately, koala bear ) is an arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia.  It is the only extant representative of the family Phascolarctidae and its closest living relatives are the wombats.  The koala is found in coastal areas of the mainland's eastern and southern regions, inhabiting Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia.  It is easily recognisable by its stout, tailless body and large head with round, fluffy ears and large, spoon-shaped nose.  The koala has a body length of 60 – and weighs 4 – .  Pelage colour ranges from silver grey to chocolate brown.  Koalas from the northern populations are typically smaller and lighter in colour than their counterparts further south.  These populations possibly are separate subspecies, but this is disputed.

Wakaleo vanderleuri | Wakaleo vanderleuri is a species of marsupial lion that lived in Australia during the Miocene (about 16 to 10million years ago).  Being a marsupial, it is not closely related to true cats; its closest living relatives are wombats and the koala.  This diprotodont is hypothesized to have been an omnivore, eating significant amounts of meat, perhaps more like a bear in diet and habit than like an actual lion.  It lacked large canine-like fangs, producing some controversy regarding just how much it actually preyed on living animals.  The meat-eating hypothesis is based largely upon the morphology of its molar teeth.  The species is believed to have been able to hunt by leaping from trees onto its prey.

Springwood State High School | Springwood State High School is a Queensland state school located in the Logan City suburb of Springwood.  The school opened in 1977 to Grade 8 students with its official opening in 1978.  Springwood State High School is situated on 55 acre of bushland and includes a lagoon which is used by senior Biology students.  In addition to this, the school is home to many native animal species including Koalas, Wallabies and many species of birds.
Muramura | "Muramura williamsi" is an extinct marsupial, related to the modern koala and wombat.  Around the size of a dog, it was a herbivore.
Ngapakaldia | Ngapakaldia is an extinct genus of marsupial, related to the modern koala and wombat.  Around the size of a sheep, it was a ground-dwelling herbivore.

Phalangeridae | The Phalangeridae are a family of nocturnal marsupials native to Australia, New Guinea and Eastern Indonesia, including the cuscuses, brushtail possums, and their close relatives.  Considered a type of possum, most species are arboreal, and they inhabit a wide range of forest habitats from alpine woodland to eucalypt forest and tropical jungle.
response.summary_1: Passage evidence: (1) "The koala ... is an arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia" and "its closest living relatives are the wombats." (Passage 2). (2) "the school ... is home to many native animal species including Koalas" (Passage 4). Bridge entity: Koala. No further retrieval needed to connect the described marsupial to Springwood State High School.
hop2_docs: Springwood State High School | Springwood State High School is a Queensland state school located in the Logan City suburb of Springwood.  The school opened in 1977 to Grade 8 students with its official opening in 1978.  Springwood State High School is situated on 55 acre of bushland and includes a lagoon which is used by senior Biology students.  In addition to this, the school is home to many native animal species including Koalas, Wallabies and many species of birds.
Springwood High School (Alabama) | Springwood High School, located in Lanett, Alabama, United States, is a private school serving around 300 students in grades K4-12.  Founded in 1970.

Springwood High School, King's Lynn | Springwood High School is a secondary school with academy status in the coastal town of King's Lynn in Norfolk, England.  It was formed by the merger of several schools when the government began to abolish the tripartite system in the mid-1960s.  Springwood has a Sixth Form with over 300 pupils and has been designated a specialist arts college and teacher training school.

Springwood High School (New South Wales) | Springwood High School is a public high school situated in the City of Blue Mountains, New South Wales.  Despite its name, the school is actually located in Grose Road, Faulconbridge.  The school was established in 1967.  Its motto is "Press Onward."

James River High School (Buchanan, Virginia) | James River High School (JRHS) is a Group-2A-size public high school in Buchanan, Virginia.  It serves the northern part of Botetourt County, including the towns of Buchanan, Eagle Rock, Fincastle, and Springwood.  James River was founded in 1959 with the consolidation of the Buchanan, Eagle Rock, and Fincastle high schools.

St Columba's High School, New South Wales | St Columba's High School, or St Columba's Catholic College, is a Roman Catholic, co-educational, secondary day school, located on the border of Winmalee and Springwood, in New South Wales, Australia.

Springwood, Queensland | Springwood is a suburb of Logan City, Queensland, Australia.  The size of Springwood is approximately 6 km².  It has 17 parks covering nearly 9% of the total area.  The population of Springwood in 1996 was 9,738 people.  By 2001 the population was 9,084 showing a population decline of 7% in the area during that time.  A high proportion of people in Springwood are in the baby boomers generation compared to population proportions in the surrounding local government area.
hop2_titles: St Columba's High School, New South Wales
response.summary_2: Springwood State High School is home to koalas (Passage 1). Koala: an arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia; closest living relatives are the wombats.
